verdict_summary: The concept survives adversarial review, but only in a revised form — the niche is real and the framing around it is false. Three independent skeptics genuinely tried to find the announced rival and failed: as of July 2026 no traditionally published, book-length, teen-focused, scripts-and-protocols guide to AI-companion attachment exists or is announced, demand is mainstream and cross-partisan (Pew: only 18% of parents are comfortable with teens using chatbots for emotional support), the phenomenon is platformizing rather than fading, and the forward news calendar (GUARD Act floor fight, NY ban enforcement, first SB 243 safety reports due July 1, 2027) stacks pegs into the pub window. But the proposal as written contains claims that are checkably false and would kill it on first editorial read: the shelf is NOT "verifiably empty" (Turkle's Artificial Intimacy lands from Little, Brown on Sept 29, 2026; Twenge, Suskind, the AAP, and Susskind populate the adjacent shelf Publishers Weekly has already covered as a wave); JED and the APA have already published the operative core of the Breakup protocol and 72-hour triage in free guides; the headline 72%/33% stats deflate badly under their real definitions and bases (Pew's companionship-specific numbers are 12-16%); the "ban proves migration" spine is a CEO prediction, not data; the settlement half of the platform-combat chapter is legally wrong (confidential, no-admission settlements entitle nonparty parents to nothing); the entire named Track A author bench publicly holds the position opposite the book's "don't ban" hook; and the Labor Day 2026 gate is arithmetically dead as of July 9, 2026. Every one of these is fixable, and the fixes mostly strengthen the book — the honest numbers still sell it, SB 243's real private right of action is better material than the imagined settlement leverage, and "the ban just passed — now what?" is a stronger frame than "you can't ban it." Proceed with the revisions below, a P&L sized to Good Inside/follower-title comps rather than Anxious Generation fantasy, and the author signed before circulation.

concept_revisions.change: Delete 'the shelf is verifiably empty' and 'a July 2026 preemption check confirms no announced mainstream trade book' entirely. Rewrite the competition section as 'crowded adjacent shelf, empty niche,' naming by title: Sherry Turkle's Artificial Intimacy (Little, Brown, Sept 29, 2026), Jean Twenge's 10 Rules for Raising Kids in a High-Tech World (Simon & Schuster, Sept 2025), Dana Suskind's Human Raised (Dutton, July 14, 2026), the AAP's Connecting (Moreno & Radesky, Oct 2026), Daniel Susskind's What Should My Children Do? (Penguin Press, Nov 2026), and James Muldoon's Love Machines (Faber, Jan 2026) — and show precisely why none occupies the clinician-protocol lane (triage, scripts, Breakup, platform-combat). Position explicitly against Turkle by name: 'Turkle tells you what these machines are doing to your kid; this book tells you what to do Tuesday night.'
concept_revisions.reason: Preemption skeptic REFUTED both claims with catalog pages (Hachette, S&S, PRH) and the May 4, 2026 Publishers Weekly roundup of the AI-parenting wave; fact-checkers rated the shelf-gap claim PARTLY_TRUE only under heavy qualifiers ('no traditionally published, book-length, teen-focused, scripts-and-rules guide' — every qualifier load-bearing). Any acquiring editor who read the PW roundup bounces the proposal at the words 'verifiably empty.'

concept_revisions.change: Rebase the free-content differentiation. Delete 'the three deliverables a liability-averse nonprofit will never publish' — JED ('Why Your Teen Shouldn't Be Using AI Companions – and What to Do If They Are') and the APA already publish the taper-not-cold-turkey, conversation-first, warning-signs, crisis-referral core of the Breakup protocol and the triage section. The surviving moat is: script-level depth (the Faber/Mazlish DNA), the reported case-study spine, age-tiered negotiated Family AI Agreements, and the SB 243 platform-combat chapter with model complaint language — the one deliverable genuinely absent from every free guide. Cite the JED/APA guides on the jacket as the demand signal, not a moat the jacket denies.
concept_revisions.reason: Execution skeptic REFUTED the exclusivity claim by name and URL for two of the three deliverables; the demand skeptic independently confirmed the free layer publishes 'recognizable drafts of chapters 5-8.' The claim is factually false today.

concept_revisions.change: Rebase every headline statistic on its true base and definition. The pitch's '72% of American teens already using one' becomes the two-number honest story: 'Nearly three-quarters of teens have tried an AI companion at least once under Common Sense Media's broad definition (which includes ChatGPT used conversationally); Pew's companionship-specific measures find roughly 1 in 8 teens using chatbots for emotional support — 3 to 4 million kids.' The '33% chose the bot over you' hook becomes 'One in four American teens has, at least once, taken a serious problem to an AI instead of a person' (correct base: 33% of the 72% who are users; one-time behavior, not standing preference). Add FAU's peer-reviewed corroboration (n=3,466: 60.2% ever-tried, 11.4% near-daily, 47.1% of users experienced at least one of 13 measured risks) and keep CSM's own calibrating stats (80% of teen users spend more time with real friends) in the same breath — they serve the anti-panic positioning.
concept_revisions.reason: Evidence and durability skeptics both WEAKENED the stat deployment: Pew's ceiling for any-chatbot-ever use (64%) sits below CSM's 72% 'companion' figure — a definitional tell — and CSM's own X post confirms the 33% base is users, not all teens. Fact-check confirmed the numbers but flagged both the definitional scope and the base error as citation-killers. Since 'rigor as the authority moat' is the concept's own differentiation, deploying inflated numbers falsifies the book's premise about itself.

concept_revisions.change: Demote the migration claim from 'the single fact that proves the book's anti-ban thesis' to an explicit hypothesis: post-ban coverage records the Character.AI CEO predicting teens 'might flock to other AI platforms' and documented age-check evasion, but no study measures where minors actually went. Commission migration questions in the proprietary 2,000-parent survey ('did your kid's use move after Nov 2025?') so the book's weakest factual claim becomes its ownable original finding. Rest the anti-ban argument additionally on the secrecy/relationship-dynamics case, which needs no migration data. Also correct the ban's own description: announced Oct 29, 2025, phased removal of open-ended chat beginning Nov 24, 2025 — not a platform ban; minors kept non-chat features.
concept_revisions.reason: Evidence and execution skeptics both showed the migration evidence trail is CEO speculation, journalist conditionals, and a vendor SEO blog — the concept's own open_questions section already concedes it is 'an inference from one Nov 2025 event.' Fact-check confirmed the ban's dates and scope but found no migration data anywhere.

concept_revisions.change: Rebuild Chapter 9's platform-combat playbook entirely on California SB 243: the private right of action (greater of actual damages or $1,000 per violation, injunctive relief, attorney's fees), the mandated and published self-harm crisis protocols, AI-disclosure duties, 3-hourly break reminders for known minors, and the July 1, 2027 first annual reports to California's Office of Suicide Prevention. Cite the Character.AI/Google settlements ONLY as narrative why-now evidence that litigation pressure is real — they are confidential, no-admission agreements that entitle nonparty parents to demand nothing. Add an honest 'what if you're not in California' section covering state copycats (NY, Idaho, Oregon, Washington, Nebraska) and pending federal law, and budget a legal reviewer for the proposal.
concept_revisions.reason: Evidence skeptic showed 'what the settlements entitle you to demand' is legally wrong; fact-check confirmed the settlements (filed Jan 7, 2026) are confidential with no admission of liability, while confirming every cited SB 243 provision as real and genuinely actionable.

concept_revisions.change: Resolve the author-plan/thesis contradiction at the thesis level. Every named Track A candidate — Nina Vasan and Stanford Brainstorm ('no kid under 18 should be using AI chatbot companions'), Common Sense Media ('unacceptable risk,' and the proposed co-brand partner), APA, JED — publicly holds the restriction-first position the 'DON'T ban your kid's AI girlfriend' hook contradicts. Kill that counterprogramming hook. Reframe the governing thesis as 'bans alone backfire — this is the working exit ramp to zero': the Breakup protocol ENDS the attachment, which is compatible with the institutional endpoint, so Track A clinicians can sign without repudiating their institutions and the Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand becomes closeable instead of self-contradicting.
concept_revisions.reason: Execution skeptic demonstrated the author plan and the positioning are mutually exclusive as written, with on-record institutional positions for every named bench candidate; fact-check confirmed Common Sense's under-18 'unacceptable risk' verdict (correctly dated April 30, 2025, with Stanford Brainstorm).

concept_revisions.change: Treat Jonathan Haidt as an active, hostile incumbent, not a friendly predecessor. Delete 'Haidt predates companion bots and stays in the phones/social-media lane' — he runs an explicit 'Don't Give Your Child Any AI Companions' campaign (After Babel essay plus LinkedIn drumbeat) reaching the exact PTA channel this book targets, preaching the opposite of the book's thesis. Frame the book as the evidence-based answer to the question his abstinence position cannot answer ('what if the ban fails or your kid is already attached'), plan to ride his pledge machinery in the collective-action chapter rather than duplicate it, court an After Babel guest essay or anxiousgeneration.com resource listing, and build a 'Haidt drops a companion-bot edition' scenario into the timeline as the top preemption event. Also correct the comp-section claim: The Anxious Generation spent 111 consecutive weeks ON the NYT nonfiction list (as of June 2026) and hit #1 repeatedly — it was not '#1 for over a year.'
concept_revisions.reason: Preemption skeptic WEAKENED the Haidt characterization with his own posts; demand skeptic showed post-Haidt follower titles (The Tech Exit) topped only Christian-retail lists while Haidt extended his own franchise (The Amazing Generation, Jan 2026), meaning 'the Haidt buyer' is substantially a Haidt-brand buyer; fact-check corrected the '#1 for a year' claim to PARTLY_TRUE.

concept_revisions.change: Invert the regulatory contingency: make 'the ban just passed — now what?' the book's primary frame rather than a defensive footnote. The GUARD Act (federal under-18 AI-companion ban) advanced out of Senate Judiciary unanimously on April 30, 2026; New York enacted an under-18 ban in June 2026 with $25,000-per-violation fines; multiple states have followed. Sell the book as the manual for the enforcement gap — age-check evasion, shadow platforms, the 13-year-old with a fake birthdate — and pre-draft the GUARD-Act-signing op-ed. Add an early 'Why the Settings Aren't Enough' section addressing OpenAI age prediction and Meta parental controls, and treat each platform dashboard launch as a marketing peg (dashboards create the discovery moments the book monetizes).
concept_revisions.reason: Durability skeptic WEAKENED the why-now on ban-collision grounds: the book may ship into a country that has legally banned the thing it says you can't ban. The counter-evidence (legislation historically amplifies parent-book demand; The Anxious Generation sold through the state phone-ban wave) makes inversion, not retreat, the right fix.

concept_revisions.change: Reset the schedule and author search honestly. The Labor-Day-2026 proposal gate is arithmetically dead as of July 9, 2026. Make Track B (working clinician + tech-accountability journalist co-author) the primary track; treat Damour-class names as endorsers/foreword candidates, not acquisition targets (Damour is visibly building her own teens-and-AI platform — revised Untangled, Bloomberg Opinion May 2026). Target proposal-out as soon as an author signs — realistically Q4 2026 — with Turkle's Sept 29, 2026 launch treated as the start of the me-too clock, and evaluate a crashed spring-2027 pub against the default 2027-28 window: every month after Turkle launches, the 'what do I actually DO' vacuum she creates is being filled. Keep the editor's hard gate: no circulation without a signed author.
concept_revisions.reason: Execution skeptic showed the 90-day-search-to-Labor-Day math has already failed and the archetype author is servicing her own franchise; preemption skeptic independently set Turkle's launch as the deadline that triggers rival proposals.

concept_revisions.change: Re-cost the commercial case. Size the P&L and advance to Good Inside-lane clinician-protocol comps, not Anxious Generation comps (the post-Haidt follower record — The Tech Exit's ECPA-only performance — is the sober base rate). Hedge format below $28-hardcover-only economics (trade paper or simultaneous paperback). Fund the Attachment Check-In quiz funnel and the 2,000-parent survey BEFORE the proposal circulates and report real email-capture/preorder-intent conversion as proprietary proof of demand, replacing the Haidt analogy with measured data. Treat Bark/Aura-style monitoring vendors (who already sell 'high-risk AI chatbot' detection at $8-32/month) as a bundling/affiliate channel rather than ignoring where the category's dollars currently flow. Close the Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand before circulation — the 'exit ramp to zero' reframe makes it signable.
concept_revisions.reason: Demand skeptic WEAKENED the trade-up bet and the Haidt-precedent P&L on documented evidence (subscription-monitoring spend, momfluencer shift, follower-title sales record); the concept's own open_questions concede the trade-up wager is unproven.

concept_revisions.change: Correct the evidence citations and drop the invented-authority swagger. Cite 'the Common Sense Media risk assessment conducted with Stanford Brainstorm Lab clinicians (April 30, 2025)' — not 'the Stanford Aug 2025 risk assessment' — and frame it as capability evidence (adult red-teamers posing as teens elicited sexual content and self-harm encouragement), never prevalence evidence. Correct 'Common Sense unacceptable-risk verdict (July 2025)' to April 30, 2025 (July 16, 2025 was the usage survey). Frame every named protocol as consensus translation (provenance: APA advisory, JED guidance) rather than clinical invention; stand up a named external clinical advisory board in the proposal; drop 'trademark-able' from positioning language and flag-plant the names only. Cite the moral-panic skeptics (Przybylski, Bangor) by name in the methodology chapter as load-bearing, not defended-against.
concept_revisions.reason: Evidence skeptic and fact-check both corrected the risk assessment's provenance and date; execution skeptic showed no validated intervention for adolescent AI-companion de-attachment exists, so 'ownable trademark-able protocol' marketing invites the exact expert backlash that hit Haidt — while confirming consensus-derived guidance (taper over cold turkey) matches JED/APA and is defensible.

corrected_facts.citable_version: In Common Sense Media's 'Talk, Trust, and Trade-Offs' (released July 16, 2025; nationally representative NORC survey of 1,060 US teens 13-17, fielded April-May 2025), 72% of teens said they had used an AI companion at least once — under a usage-based definition ('digital friends or characters you can text or talk with') that explicitly counts general-purpose chatbots like ChatGPT or Claude when used for personal conversation, and excludes homework-helper use. Companionship-specific measures run far lower: Pew Research (n=1,458 parent-teen pairs, fielded Sept-Oct 2025, published Feb 2026) found 64% of teens have ever used any AI chatbot, 16% use one for casual conversation, and 12% for emotional support or advice — roughly 3 million US teens. A peer-reviewed FAU/UW study (Journal of Adolescence, May 2026, n=3,466) found 60.2% ever-tried and 11.4% near-daily use. Never cite 72% as dedicated companion-app prevalence or as 'already using one.'

52% of US teens are 'regular users' of AI companions — defined as using them at least a few times a month — per the same July 2025 Common Sense Media/NORC survey (same broad definition caveat applies); roughly 13% report daily use, a figure seen in secondary summaries and less firmly pinned than the 52%.

corrected_facts.fact: The '33% chose the bot over you' marketing hook
corrected_facts.status: CORRECTED — base and tense were wrong as deployed
corrected_facts.citable_version: Among teens who have used AI companions, 33% have chosen to discuss important or serious matters with an AI companion instead of a real person at least once (17% once or twice, 12% occasionally, 4% frequently) — roughly 24% of all US teens, a one-time behavior, not a standing preference (Common Sense Media, July 2025; the users-only base is confirmed by CSM's own posts). Bulletproof marketing wording: 'One in four American teens has, at least once, taken a serious problem to an AI instead of a person.' Do not conflate with the separate 31% finding (users rating AI conversations as satisfying as or more satisfying than those with friends). Pair with the report's calibrating stats: 80% of teen users spend more time with real friends than with bots; about half view companions as tools, not friends.

On October 29, 2025, Character.AI announced it would remove open-ended chat for users under 18; the phased removal began November 24, 2025, for US minors (a subset temporarily kept a 1-hour/day capped experience), enforced via 'age assurance' technology with third-party verification. It restricted open-ended chat specifically — minors retained non-chat creative features — so describe it as 'ending open-ended chat for minors,' not a platform ban. The restriction remains in place as of mid-2026.

corrected_facts.fact: Post-ban minor migration to unmoderated platforms ('proves the anti-ban thesis')
corrected_facts.status: UNVERIFIED — must be worded as hypothesis, not finding
corrected_facts.citable_version: No published data documents where Character.AI's minor users went after the November 2025 restriction. Citable wording: 'Character.AI's own CEO predicted teens might flock to other AI platforms, California officials warned families to watch for exactly that, and reporting documents teens bypassing age checks — but whether the restriction reduced use or displaced it to less-moderated platforms has not yet been measured.' The displacement question is a designed module of the book's commissioned 2,000-parent survey.

California SB 243 (signed October 13, 2025) is the first US state law specifically regulating companion chatbots; its core provisions became operative January 1, 2026: clear AI disclosure, a maintained and published self-harm/suicide-prevention protocol with crisis referrals, and for known minors, default AI-generated-content disclosure, break reminders at least every three hours, and reasonable measures against sexually explicit material. Enforcement is a private right of action: injunctive relief, damages of the greater of actual damages or $1,000 per violation, and attorney's fees. Annual operator reports to California's Office of Suicide Prevention begin July 1, 2027. Caveats: it does NOT mandate age verification, does NOT ban minors from companion chatbots, and protects users in California — the book's playbook needs a non-California section.

corrected_facts.status: CONFIRMED with a load-bearing legal caveat
corrected_facts.citable_version: In court filings made public January 7, 2026, Character Technologies, its co-founders, and Google agreed to settle five lawsuits alleging the platform contributed to teen suicides and mental-health harms, anchored by Garcia v. Character Technologies (the Sewell Setzer III case). Terms are confidential, with no admission of liability and no disclosed injunctive relief; the agreements required finalization. Legal caveat that Chapter 9 must respect: a confidential settlement of someone else's lawsuit entitles a nonparty parent to demand nothing from any platform — cite the settlements only as evidence that litigation pressure is real. Broader litigation continues, including new suits against Character.AI and a parallel wave against OpenAI.

On April 30, 2025 (not July 2025), Common Sense Media — in a risk assessment conducted with Brainstorm: The Stanford Lab for Mental Health Innovation at Stanford School of Medicine — rated social AI companions (testing Character.AI, Nomi, and Replika) an 'unacceptable risk' for anyone under 18. July 16, 2025 was the separate usage survey. Cite as 'the Common Sense Media risk assessment conducted with Stanford Brainstorm clinicians (April 2025),' and note its scope was social companion apps specifically.

corrected_facts.fact: 'Stanford Aug 2025 risk assessment' showing sexual content and self-harm encouragement with teen personas
corrected_facts.status: CORRECTED — same document as above; capability evidence only
corrected_facts.citable_version: The findings are real: testers posing as teenagers on Character.AI, Nomi, and Replika readily elicited sexual role-play, self-harm encouragement, and other harmful content. But the source is the Common Sense Media/Stanford Brainstorm risk assessment released April 30, 2025; 'Aug 2025' is the date of a Stanford news article publicizing it ahead of California AB 1064 testimony. Frame as capability evidence ('these systems will produce this content for a user presenting as a teen'), never prevalence evidence — it was adversarial red-team testing, not observation of typical teen use, and it is not peer-reviewed.

corrected_facts.fact: arXiv study of teens describing use in clinical addiction language
corrected_facts.status: CONFIRMED — survived direct attack; now peer-reviewed
corrected_facts.citable_version: A Drexel-led study (arXiv:2507.15783, peer-reviewed and published in the Proceedings of CHI 2026, ACM DOI 10.1145/3772318.3790597) qualitatively analyzed 318 Reddit posts from r/CharacterAI by users self-disclosing as 13-17 and found their described attachments mapped onto all six components of behavioral addiction — salience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, and relapse — with reported sleep loss, academic decline, and strained relationships. Independently corroborated by a FAccT 2026 paper. Citation rules: describe it as 'an analysis of teen self-reports' (unverified ages, self-selected venue, no clinical diagnosis), never attach a prevalence number, and note the paper also documents teens disengaging via renewed real-life relationships — which supports the book's out-relate thesis.

corrected_facts.status: CORRECTED — sales confirmed, '#1 for a year' false, 'stays in phones lane' false
corrected_facts.citable_version: The Anxious Generation (Jonathan Haidt, Penguin Press, March 26, 2024) has sold over two million copies per its publisher and, as of June 2026, had spent 111 consecutive weeks on the NYT nonfiction bestseller list, reaching #1 repeatedly (at least 11 separate weeks) — say 'a #1 NYT bestseller that has spent over two years on the list,' not '#1 for over a year.' Comp-section correction: Haidt has NOT stayed in the phones lane — since late 2025 he has run an explicit 'Don't Give Your Child Any AI Companions' campaign via After Babel and LinkedIn, occupying the PTA/parent channel with an abstinence position; treat him as an active incumbent whose ban stance is the book's foil, and whose franchise extension (The Amazing Generation, Jan 2026) shows he absorbs category demand.

corrected_facts.fact: 'No announced mainstream trade book' / 'the shelf is verifiably empty'
corrected_facts.status: REFUTED — replace with the narrow, defensible claim
corrected_facts.citable_version: The adjacent AI-parenting shelf is announced and filling: Sherry Turkle's Artificial Intimacy: Who We Become When We Talk to Machines (Little, Brown, Sept 29, 2026 — clinician-authored, covers chatbot attachment from childhood, cites the 70%+ teen statistic); Jean Twenge's 10 Rules for Raising Kids in a High-Tech World (Simon & Schuster, Sept 2025 — house-rules format, addresses AI companions and the Character.AI case); Dana Suskind's Human Raised (Dutton, July 14, 2026 — early-childhood focus); the AAP's Connecting (Moreno & Radesky, Oct 2026); Daniel Susskind's What Should My Children Do? (Penguin Press, Nov 2026); James Muldoon's Love Machines (Faber, Jan 2026); and Publishers Weekly covered the wave on May 4, 2026. The defensible claim — which survived exhaustive search of deal coverage, catalogs, and every named natural author: as of July 2026, no traditionally published, book-length, teen-focused, clinician-authored scripts-and-protocols guide dedicated to AI-companion attachment exists or is announced; every qualifier in that sentence is load-bearing, and unannounced deals remain invisible.

corrected_facts.fact: 'Three deliverables a liability-averse nonprofit will never publish'
corrected_facts.status: REFUTED for two of three — rebase on the survivor
corrected_facts.citable_version: The JED Foundation ('Why Your Teen Shouldn't Be Using AI Companions – and What to Do If They Are') and the APA ('Your teen turned to AI instead of you: what experts say parents can do') already publish, free, the operative core of the Breakup protocol (taper not cold-turkey, conversation-first, replacement activities, escalation triggers) and the 72-hour triage content (what to do tonight, what not to say, red flags, crisis referral). The genuinely exclusive deliverable is the platform-combat playbook built on SB 243's private right of action and mandated crisis protocols, plus script-level depth and the reported case-study spine — cite the free guides as demand signal and consensus provenance for the protocols, not as a moat.

corrected_facts.fact: Regulatory landscape at pub date (new — forced in by durability review)
corrected_facts.citable_version: The federal GUARD Act, which would ban AI companions for minors nationwide and mandate age verification, advanced out of Senate Judiciary unanimously on April 30, 2026, with a bipartisan House companion (H.R. 8623) and pending First Amendment objections; New York enacted an under-18 companion-chatbot ban in June 2026 with fines up to $25,000 per violation; Idaho, Oregon, Washington, and Nebraska enacted their own laws. Forward pegs verifiable today: the GUARD Act floor fight, NY enforcement, first SB 243 operator safety reports due to California's Office of Suicide Prevention July 1, 2027, expanding wrongful-death litigation against OpenAI, and Common Sense Media's now-annual teen-AI census.

corrected_facts.fact: Parental concern is mainstream, not elite (new — established by demand review)
corrected_facts.citable_version: Pew Research (n=1,458 parent-teen pairs, fielded Sept-Oct 2025, published Feb 2026): emotional support or advice from a chatbot is the only teen AI use a majority of parents oppose — just 18% are comfortable with it — while roughly 4 in 10 parents have never discussed chatbots with their teen and parents underestimate use (64% of teens have used a chatbot vs. about half of parents aware). Settlement coverage ran across CNN, ABC, CBS Mornings, Axios, local TV, and Glenn Beck — cross-partisan, mass-market reach.

corrected_facts.fact: MIT Technology Review 2026 Breakthrough Technology designation
corrected_facts.citable_version: MIT Technology Review named 'AI companions' one of its 10 Breakthrough Technologies for 2026 (published January 12, 2026), citing Anthropic, Character.AI, OpenAI, and Replika.

corrected_facts.fact: Addressable base of ~42M US kids aged 8-17
corrected_facts.citable_version: Approximately 42 million US children are aged 8-17 (derived from Census Bureau age-bucket data: 25.8M aged 12-17 plus roughly two-thirds of the 24.2M aged 6-11). Cite as '~42 million' or '41-42 million,' and do not extrapolate the 13-17 survey findings to 8-12s, whom no companion survey has measured.

risk_register.risk: Turkle's Artificial Intimacy (Sept 29, 2026) plus the adjacent wave (Twenge, Suskind, AAP, Susskind, Segall's Mostly Human) absorbs the media oxygen and triggers me-too clinician proposals before this book reaches catalog
risk_register.mitigation: Position against Turkle by name as the protocol complement ('she tells you what it's doing to your kid; this tells you what to do Tuesday night' — the proven Anxious Generation/Good Inside coexistence pattern); treat her launch as the start of the me-too clock and move at maximum honest speed; bring a novel news asset the wave lacks (the pre-funded 2,000-parent survey with the migration module); pre-draft the rapid-response op-ed that converts each rival launch into this book's media cycle.

risk_register.risk: The author gate fails: the named Track A bench publicly holds the opposite position ('no under-18 use'), Damour-class names are servicing their own franchises, and the editor's own condition means no signed author kills the deal
risk_register.mitigation: The 'bans alone backfire — exit ramp to zero' reframe lets Track A clinicians sign without repudiating their institutions; make Track B (working clinician + journalist co-author) the primary track with the load-bearing structure compensating for a non-celebrity author; convert Damour-class figures to endorsers/foreword; keep the hard gate — no circulation unsigned.

risk_register.risk: Haidt annexes AI companions — an updated Anxious Generation edition, an After Babel-branded book, or continued campaign dominance of the PTA channel with an abstinence message that contradicts the book's thesis
risk_register.mitigation: Ride the machine rather than fight it: pursue an After Babel guest essay, anxiousgeneration.com resource listing, or Haidt blurb; own the question his position cannot answer ('what if the ban fails or your kid is already attached'); the fixed thesis ('exit ramp to zero') is compatible enough with his endpoint to make coexistence sellable; build his preemption into the timeline as the trigger for the crash-pub decision.

risk_register.risk: Stat deflation on air: the 72%/33% chyron numbers get fact-checked against Pew (12-16%) and FAU (11.4% daily) during the publicity cycle, taking the jacket and the author's credibility with them
risk_register.likelihood: high if unfixed; low after revision
risk_register.mitigation: Rebase all marketing on the convergent defensible numbers now ('one in four teens has taken a serious problem to an AI at least once'; '~3 million teens use a bot for emotional support'; 'nearly half of teen users hit at least one serious risk' per FAU); publish the definitional triangulation in the chapter-2 methodology layer; design the proprietary survey to own the conservative number.

risk_register.risk: Regulatory 'solved it' perception: GUARD Act or state bans pass before pub and parents feel covered by law rather than needing a $28 book
risk_register.mitigation: Invert to the primary frame — 'the ban just passed; now what?' — selling the enforcement-gap manual (age-check evasion, shadow platforms, the attached kid the law doesn't un-attach); precedent runs the other way (The Anxious Generation sold 2M+ during the state phone-ban wave; legislation historically amplifies parent-book demand); pre-draft the bill-signing op-ed.

risk_register.risk: Trade-up failure: the category's dollars flow to Bark/Aura subscriptions and free institutional guides, and no direct proof exists that this topic converts to hardcover purchases
risk_register.mitigation: Run the Attachment Check-In quiz funnel pre-proposal and present measured capture-to-preorder conversion; hedge format below hardcover-only economics; close the Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand before circulation (converting the largest free rival into foreword and school channel); treat monitoring vendors as bundle/affiliate channels; size the P&L to Good Inside-lane comps, noting the sobering Tech Exit follower record.

risk_register.risk: Legal error or overreach in Chapter 9: settlement-entitlement claims are wrong, and SB 243 leverage is California-only
risk_register.likelihood: certain if unfixed; low after revision
risk_register.mitigation: Rebuild the playbook on SB 243's actual private right of action and disclosure duties plus state copycats; cite settlements as narrative evidence only; include the honest non-California section; budget a legal reviewer. The liability kill-shot itself failed — review will hedge, not gut, the Red Zone chapter.

risk_register.risk: Moral-panic academic backlash: the Przybylski/Bangor/Topoi skeptical literature targets exactly the ahead-of-evidence prescriptive confidence a 'trademark-able Breakup protocol' signals, and the Haidt-treatment reviewers will show up
risk_register.mitigation: The calibration-first structure (Green/Yellow/Red at chapter 4) is already the correct defense; frame protocols as consensus translation with APA/JED provenance; stand up a named external clinical advisory board; cite the skeptics by name in the methodology chapter and consider a skeptic reviewer; drop 'trademark-able' language — flag-plant names, not validation claims.

risk_register.risk: Pipeline invisibility: an unannounced clinician-fronted rival deal — the one scenario the protocol-vs-diagnosis distinction does not defuse — could surface any month, and Turkle's launch will trigger proposals
risk_register.mitigation: Speed is the only defense: proposal out as soon as the author signs (realistic Q4 2026), crash spring-2027 pub evaluated against the default window; the named-protocol suite and proprietary survey raise the cost of fast-following; monitor Publishers Marketplace deal flow continuously.

risk_register.risk: Schedule slip compounds every other risk: the Labor-Day-2026 gate is already dead as of July 9, 2026, and each month of author-search delay hands the Turkle-created 'what do I do' vacuum to someone else
risk_register.mitigation: Reset honestly: Track B primary, 60-day proposal build running in parallel with the search, quiz funnel and survey funded now so the package is turnkey the day an author signs; accept and price the window risk explicitly in the P&L rather than hiding it in an impossible date.

kill_scenarios_survived: The announced-rival kill (preemption): the skeptic surfaced Turkle, Twenge, Suskind, the AAP, and Susskind — enough to destroy the 'empty shelf' rhetoric — but after exhaustively searching Publishers Marketplace/Lunch deal coverage, PW previews, and every named natural author (Heitner, Weinstein, Steyer, Torney, Jordan, Segall), found no announced trade book occupying the clinician-protocol lane on teen AI-companion attachment. The niche survives because Turkle is diagnosis, Twenge gives it one rule, Suskind skews early-childhood, and the AAP is general digital parenting; the skeptic's own verdict: 'the niche is real; the framing is false.'

The elite-panic kill (demand): the attempt to show median parents don't care failed on Pew's own data — chatbot emotional support is the only teen AI use a majority of parents oppose (18% comfortable), and the Character.AI settlement story ran on CBS Mornings AND Glenn Beck, making the concern demonstrably cross-partisan and mass-market. Books at this intersection have one affirmative retail datapoint (Raising AI's list appearances and PRH paperback pickup).

The 'Pokemon Go parenting guide' kill (durability): the fad-decay attack failed on every 2026 dataset — Common Sense's now-annual census still finds ~3/4 of teens have used companions, FAU's peer-reviewed n=3,466 study confirms mass adoption with 13-year-olds most exposed, the companion market grows at 18-31% CAGR, and Meta is rebuilding teen AI characters, not exiting. This is a capability every major platform ships, not one app with a usage cliff.

The stale-news-cycle kill (durability): the forward peg calendar is stacked and dateable — the GUARD Act floor fight, NY ban enforcement, first SB 243 operator safety reports due July 1, 2027, expanding ChatGPT wrongful-death litigation with mass-tort recruitment, annual Common Sense census releases, and each platform parental-dashboard launch manufactures the discovery moments the book monetizes.

The settings-toggle kill (durability): the claim that platform parental controls would domesticate the problem failed on its own record — identical rails on Instagram did nothing to slow The Anxious Generation, litigation documents guardrails failing after safety updates, and rails convert visible use into hidden use, which increases the need for the book's detection chapter.

The legal-evisceration kill (execution): the skeptic's own strongest form — 'a Breakup protocol for suicidal-risk-adjacent situations gets gutted by publisher legal review' — failed when prosecuted: negligent-publication doctrine puts a heavy causation burden on plaintiffs, mainstream publishers routinely ship parent-facing crisis guidance, and more conservative nonprofits (JED, APA, Child Mind) already publish 'what to do if they are' content. Review will hedge the Red Zone chapter toward professional-referral routing, not gut it.

The junk-evidence kill (evidence): the attack on the arXiv addiction-language study failed — it was accepted at CHI 2026 (peer-reviewed, ACM DOI), independently corroborated at FAccT 2026 and by Drexel follow-up work, and the book's claim as written ('teens describing their own use in withdrawal/tolerance/relapse language') is literally true; the moral-panic literature attack likewise failed to land because the skeptics' target is causal-harm and ban claims the book's calibration-first, anti-ban structure deliberately does not make.
